summary: I was born in 1999 in Killeen, Texas, a small town in Southern Texas where my father was stationed with the US Army. Although I only lived there for about three months before my parents divorced, Killeen marks the very beginning of my life story. It’s interesting because even though I left so early, it’s part of the complex mix that shapes my identity.

My connection to the United States Army comes through my father, who was based in the Army when I was born in Killeen, Texas. His role meant we started life near a military community, although I left that environment very early on. The Army indirectly influenced my early years and the places I was born and lived, setting the stage for the diverse experiences that followed.

After my parents divorced, I moved with my mom to New Jersey where I was raised for a time in homeless shelters. It was a tough period, but eventually we were taken in by a kind couple who were college-educated professors. Growing up in New Jersey with them exposed me to a wide range of people from different backgrounds and sparked my curiosity about the world.

While living in New Jersey with the couple who took us in, I shared a room for a while with a family from Gambia. This experience was part of the multicultural environment that surrounded me and helped me develop a deep interest in different cultures and people. It was one of the early moments that made me want to explore the world beyond my immediate surroundings.

Thailand became a significant part of my life when the couple who raised me moved there on my 13th birthday to start an NGO in a rural village. I couldn’t see them for five years, but I was determined to repay their kindness by eventually joining them. After turning 18, I got my visa and traveled to Thailand, where I lived in Phrao, worked on rice farms, learned the language, and embraced a very different lifestyle.

Phrao is the small town in Thailand where I stayed after getting my visa at 18 to help the couple who had adopted me. Living there was a huge change from my life in the US—I worked on rice farms and adapted to life without electricity. The experience in Phrao taught me a lot about resilience, culture, and adventure, and it inspired me to keep exploring the world.

After my time in Thailand, I chose to continue my studies in Japan, driven by the adventurous spirit I developed growing up. Coming from parents who had never left the US, moving to Japan was a big step for me. I hope to keep traveling and learning from new places, just like the role models who shaped my childhood would want.
